title: Orders Conversion Baseline Classifier
goal: Build a simple, readable baseline classifier that predicts whether a user has an order conversion using only simple aggregated event features.
business_context: A product team wants a first conversion-propensity baseline before investing in complex ML. The goal is not production accuracy; the goal is to learn how to define a target, avoid leakage, evaluate honestly, and explain limitations.

related_portfolio_templates.title: Baseline classifier model card
related_portfolio_templates.what_to_write: Write a model card with problem, data, target, features, split strategy, metrics, limitations, leakage risks, and next steps. Use real metrics from your Notebook run; do not invent numbers.
related_portfolio_templates.chart_or_table: Include a metrics table, confusion matrix, and a short feature/target definition table.
related_portfolio_templates.readme_bullet: Built a conversion baseline classifier with pandas and scikit-learn, defining a target, creating aggregated features, evaluating classification metrics, and documenting leakage risks in a model card.

milestones.title: Define the target
milestones.description: Define the binary target clearly: converted = user has at least one order in df_orders.csv. Write down what one prediction row represents. For this MVP, use user-level rows. Note the main caveat: if event features include behavior after conversion, the model can leak future information.
checklist: I wrote the target definition in one sentence.
I chose user-level prediction rows.
I described at least one leakage risk.
I did not claim this is production-ready conversion prediction.
milestones.portfolio_output: Target definition paragraph for the model card.

milestones.title: Prepare the modeling table
milestones.description: Load events and orders, create one row per user, and attach the converted target. Keep the code simple and inspect shapes at every step.
dataset_hints: Use relative paths from project root: datasets/df_events.csv and datasets/df_orders.csv.
milestones.starter_code:
import pandas as pd

# Load source data from project root.
events = pd.read_csv("datasets/df_events.csv")
orders = pd.read_csv("datasets/df_orders.csv")

print("events", events.shape)
print("orders", orders.shape)
print(events.head())
print(orders.head())

# One row per user from events.
users = events[["user_id"]].drop_duplicates().copy()
converted_users = orders[["user_id"]].drop_duplicates().assign(converted=1)
model_df = users.merge(converted_users, on="user_id", how="left")
model_df["converted"] = model_df["converted"].fillna(0).astype(int)

print(model_df["converted"].value_counts(normalize=True))
print(model_df.head())
checklist: I loaded both datasets from datasets/.
model_df has one row per user_id.
converted is binary 0/1.
I checked the class balance.

milestones.title: Create simple features
milestones.description: Create only simple aggregated event features. Start with counts: total events and event_type counts per user. Exclude direct conversion/post-purchase events such as order.created from baseline features, because they leak the target. Keep feature names readable.
dataset_hints: Use groupby and pivot_table/crosstab on df_events.csv.
milestones.starter_code:
# Simple count features.
# Exclude event types that directly encode conversion or post-purchase behavior.
leaky_event_types = {"order.created", "support.feedback_submitted"}
feature_events = events[~events["event_type"].isin(leaky_event_types)].copy()

print("excluded event types:", sorted(leaky_event_types & set(events["event_type"].unique())))

event_counts = feature_events.groupby("user_id").size().rename("events_total")

if "event_type" in feature_events.columns:
    event_type_features = pd.crosstab(feature_events["user_id"], feature_events["event_type"])
    event_type_features.columns = [f"event_type__{col}" for col in event_type_features.columns]
else:
    event_type_features = pd.DataFrame(index=event_counts.index)

features = pd.concat([event_counts, event_type_features], axis=1).fillna(0).reset_index()
model_df = model_df.merge(features, on="user_id", how="left").fillna(0)

feature_cols = [col for col in model_df.columns if col not in {"user_id", "converted"}]
print("features", feature_cols)
print(model_df[feature_cols + ["converted"]].head())
checklist: I created at least one numeric feature.
Features are aggregated by user_id.
I can explain each feature in plain language.
I excluded order.created or clearly post-purchase events from baseline features.
I noted whether any remaining feature could still leak future behavior.

milestones.title: Make a train/test split
milestones.description: Split features and target into train/test sets. Use stratification so class balance is similar in both parts. This is the first honest evaluation guardrail.
milestones.starter_code:
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X = model_df[feature_cols]
y = model_df["converted"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X,
    y,
    test_size=0.25,
    random_state=42,
    stratify=y,
)

print("train", X_train.shape, y_train.mean())
print("test", X_test.shape, y_test.mean())
checklist: I split before evaluating.
I used stratify=y.
I checked target rate in train and test.
I did not tune decisions on the test set repeatedly.
milestones.portfolio_output: Train/test split note for the model card.

milestones.title: Train a baseline model
milestones.description: Train a simple LogisticRegression baseline. The point is a transparent baseline before complexity, not maximum score.
dataset_hints: Use sklearn.linear_model.LogisticRegression and sklearn.pipeline/StandardScaler if needed.
milestones.starter_code:
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

baseline = make_pipeline(
    StandardScaler(with_mean=False),
    LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, class_weight="balanced", random_state=42),
)

baseline.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred = baseline.predict(X_test)
y_proba = baseline.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

print("trained baseline")
print("positive predictions", int(y_pred.sum()))
checklist: I trained only on X_train/y_train.
I used a simple baseline model.
I can explain why baseline comes before complex models.
I saved predictions for evaluation.

milestones.title: Evaluate classification metrics
milestones.description: Calculate classification metrics. Include accuracy, precision, recall, F1, ROC AUC if both classes are present, and confusion matrix. Interpret what each metric means for conversion prediction.
milestones.starter_code:
from sklearn.metrics import (
    accuracy_score,
    precision_score,
    recall_score,
    f1_score,
    roc_auc_score,
    confusion_matrix,
    classification_report,
)

metrics = {
    "accuracy": accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred),
    "precision": precision_score(y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
    "recall": recall_score(y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
    "f1": f1_score(y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
}
if y_test.nunique() == 2:
    metrics["roc_auc"] = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_proba)

print(metrics)
print(confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred))
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0))
checklist: I reported more than accuracy.
I inspected the confusion matrix.
I explained precision vs recall tradeoff.
I did not present metrics as product impact.
milestones.portfolio_output: Metrics table and confusion matrix for the model card.

milestones.title: Do basic error analysis
milestones.description: Inspect false positives and false negatives. Look for simple patterns, but keep conclusions careful because this is a baseline with simple features.
dataset_hints: Join predictions back to user_id for inspection.
milestones.starter_code:
eval_df = X_test.copy()
eval_df["user_id"] = model_df.loc[X_test.index, "user_id"].values
eval_df["y_true"] = y_test.values
eval_df["y_pred"] = y_pred
eval_df["score"] = y_proba

false_positives = eval_df[(eval_df["y_true"] == 0) & (eval_df["y_pred"] == 1)]
false_negatives = eval_df[(eval_df["y_true"] == 1) & (eval_df["y_pred"] == 0)]

print("false positives", false_positives.shape)
print("false negatives", false_negatives.shape)
print(false_positives.head())
print(false_negatives.head())
checklist: I counted false positives and false negatives.
I inspected several examples.
I wrote one hypothesis for each error type.
I stated what extra data/time split would improve the analysis.

milestones.title: Write the model card
milestones.description: Create a concise model card. It should be honest enough for a portfolio: what problem you modeled, what data you used, what target/features mean, what metrics you got, where leakage may exist, and what you would do next.
milestones.reflection_prompt: Write the model card from your real Notebook outputs. Do not invent metrics. Keep leakage risk and baseline limitations explicit.
dataset_hints: Use real metrics from your Notebook output.
milestones.starter_code:
model_card_template = """
# Orders Conversion Baseline Classifier

## Problem
What decision or learning question does this baseline support?

## Data
Which datasets were used? What is one row?

## Target
How is converted defined?

## Features
Which simple aggregated features were used?

## Metrics
Paste real metrics from your evaluation. Do not invent numbers.

## Limitations
Leakage risks, class balance, time split limitations, missing context.

## Next steps
Better time-aware split, richer features, threshold tuning, calibration, product validation.
"""
print(model_card_template)
checklist: The model card has problem, data, target, features, metrics, limitations, and next steps.
Metrics are copied from a real run.
Leakage risk is explicit.
The writeup says baseline, not production model.

deliverables: Notebook with target definition, features, split, baseline model, and metrics
portfolio_prompt: Write a model-card style portfolio case study. Use your real metrics, explain target and leakage risks, show why the model is a baseline, and list concrete next steps.
